claims.text: In summer 2023 a remote Amazon village was repeatedly attacked by 7-foot-tall humanoid 'Pelacaras' (face peelers) in black armor and yellow-eyed helmets that hovered silently above the ground, were impervious to shotgun fire, and tried to peel the faces off victims like 15-year-old Tia.

evidence.quote:
girl started screaming.
The men on patrol responded quickly.
Over the past few weeks, something had
been terrorizing their village, and
tonight they were ready. The men, armed
with hunting rifles and shotguns, opened
fire. The bullets had no effect. The
attackers were 7 ft tall, covered in
black armor with large helmets and
glowing yellow eyes. In an instant, they
were gone. But they didn't run. They
hovered 6 ft off the ground and
disappeared into the Amazon jungle.
The girl was bleeding from a cut on her
neck and barely conscious. She almost
became another victim of los pelicaras,
the face peelers. At first, the
authorities didn't beli

claims.rationale: The Peruvian Loreto-region 'Pelacaras' attacks of July-August 2023 were a real, widely-reported social phenomenon and the Peruvian Navy did deploy a unit. Witnesses (including Tia Davila Diaz) consistently described tall hovering attackers in black armor. What's contested is the explanation: the Peruvian government and most journalists settled on either illegal-miners or mass psychogenic episode; alien-tech and human-trafficking theories remain unproven. The events-as-experienced are well-attested; the literal '7-ft hovering aliens' framing is the host's framing.

claims.text: These 2023 attacks were not new — Brazil, Colombia and Peru have had violent encounters for decades, including the 1970s Operation Prato in Colares where over 200 victims were attacked by mysterious light beams that drained their blood (two died), documented by the Brazilian Air Force.

evidence.quote:
acks in 2023 were shocking, but
they weren't new. For years, something
has been hunting people in the Amazon.
In the 1970s, the island Coladis in
Brazil was under siege. People were
attacked by beams of light that drained
their blood. They all had burn marks and
puncture wounds that doctors couldn't
explain. There were over 200 victims.
Two people even died. Finally, the mayor
begged the government for help. The
Brazilian Air Force took thousands of
photographs, videos, and witness
testimonies. That was Operation Pr.
There's a link below to the whole story.
It can't be debunked. And there's a
twist. But in case you haven't seen it,
I won't spoil it. Brazil, Colombi

claims.rationale: Operation Prato (1977-78) in Colares, Pará state, Brazil, is a real and well-documented Brazilian Air Force investigation into UFO/'chupa-chupa' light-beam attacks on villagers. The 200+ victim count, burn marks, puncture wounds, and the Brazilian Air Force's photographic/witness records are accurate; the official report was declassified in 2005. Two villager deaths were attributed to the events but causation (vs. underlying medical causes) remains contested. The host's framing is largely correct.

claims.text: The earliest documented Pelacaras case was Juan Cervantes in 2013, found floating in Lake Amria with no signs of drowning or trauma but his entire face removed down to the skull and a precise surgical cut around his neck — and an autopsy showed he died before entering the water, ruling out the official 'piranhas' explanation.

evidence.quote:
and
Peru kept having encounters, all
violent. The earliest documented case
was Juan Cervantes in 2013. His body was
found floating in Lake Amria. No signs
of drowning or trauma, but his face was
completely gone, right down to the
skull, and there was a precise cut all
around his neck. Skeptics said he was
attacked by piranhas, but the autopsy
showed he died before he went into the
water. Fish would have eaten more than
just his face, and fish don't make
surgical cuts, but the face peelers do.
In April 2023, the face peelers attacked
again. A boy's body was found in the
same area. Again, his face was gone.
Again, precise cuts. There's a video of
this if you want to see it, which you
don't.
The attacks then acce

claims.rationale: The Cervantes case is documented in regional Peruvian/Colombian press from 2013-2014 as one of several Amazon-region body-found-with-face-missing reports. The autopsy details and cause-of-death sequencing have been variously reported and not fully released publicly; the 'died before water entry, surgical cuts' framing is consistent with some accounts but not the only published version. Real case; the 'too clean for piranhas' inference is reasonable but not definitively established.

claims.text: On September 2, 2023 villager Pablo Verdales saw 7-foot humanoids in black astronaut-like armor hovering on disc-shaped platforms only 50 feet from his Pakuna Lake home; when he hit one with a shotgun the armor sparked but the entity was unaffected, then it knocked him out for hours with a laser beam before they accelerated up into the night sky.

evidence.quote:
lerated. On
September 2nd, 2023, Pablo Verdales, a
villager from Pakuna Lake, was sitting
on his porch. About 7 at night, he saw a
light between the trees only 50 ft from
his house. He called his family out and
everyone saw the same thing. Humanoid
beings about 7t tall. They wore black
astronautlike armor. They wore black
helmets that hid their features except
for glowing yellow eyes. And most
shocking, they stood on discshaped
platforms and hovered above the ground.
Pablo fired his shotgun and hit one of
them. Except for a few sparks from the
impact on the armor, the entity was
unaffected. Then it returned fire. It
shot a laser beam that knocked Pablo out
for hours. Then the beings accelerated
into the air and disappeared into the
night sky. There was no doubt this was
yet another alien attack in the Amazon.
Not only did the villagers see the

were acorn-shaped, the length of a
private jet, but wider. No wings, no
propellers, just bumps on a hull and a
lot of flashing lights. The major hot
spot was San Antonio Depinyaku, a remote
village in the jungle occupied by the
indigenou

claims.rationale: Pablo Verdales is a real named witness whose testimony is part of the regional press record from Sept 2023. The level of physical detail (sparks on armor, laser beam knockout, hovering disks) is hard to corroborate independently; eyewitness reports under stress in low-light conditions are notoriously unreliable. The event-witnessed-and-reported is real; the literal interpretation is what's contested.

claims.text: On July 11, 2023, 15-year-old Talia 'Tia' Davila Diaz was attacked in her backyard by two humanoid figures who sedated her with a nasal syringe, smeared cold cream on her face to numb it, used a laser scalpel on her neck, and were heard speaking Spanish — one with a local Peruvian accent, the other with an American 'gringo' accent — saying 'don't use too much, you'll ruin the flesh' before the villagers scared them off.

evidence.quote:
15-year-old
Tia was attacked. After she escaped, she
told her family exactly what the aliens
wanted. They wanted her face. They asked
how she knew this. She said, "I heard
them say it.
On July 11th, 2023, Talia Dabila Diaz
was in her backyard picking fruit, as
she did almost every night. Suddenly,
she felt a rush of wind behind her. She
turned around and saw a tall figure
hovering on a circular platform. It was
covered in black armor and had bright
yellow eyes. It moved silently and
faster than any human she'd ever seen.
Then another being grabbed her from
behind and covered her mouth. The first
held her legs. She fought, but their
grip was unnaturally strong. Then the
attack became a procedure. One of the
figures put something like a nasal
syringe up her nose. Instantly, Tia was
overwhelmed by a sharp chemical smell.
Her vision began to blur as she started
to lose consciousness. They had given
her some kind of seditive. Then they
smeared cold cream on her face.
Terrified and disoriented, her face
started going numb. Then she heard them
speaking, speaking Spanish. One had a
local Peruvian accent. The other had an
American accent, a gringo. She heard the
local voice say, "Be careful. Be
careful." Then he said the words that
defined this entire phenomenon. Don't
use too much. You'll ruin the flesh.
They weren't trying to kill her. They
were trying to peel her face. One of
them produced a small glowing device, a
laser scalpel. She heard a humming sound
and smelled something burning. They were
cutting into the side of her neck.
Somehow she was able to get her hand
under the helmet of one of her
attackers. This gave her just enough
time to scream. Then she heard the local
attacker say, "Let's get out of here."
Then the Americans said, "No, we can't
just leave her here." They tried to drag
her onto one of the platforms, but the

claims.rationale: Tia (Talia) Davila Diaz is a real named survivor whose July 11 2023 testimony to local press, Peruvian Navy investigators, and later Tim Alberino is the central case in the entire Pelacaras phenomenon. The 'Don't use too much, you'll ruin the flesh' (No usen tanto, van a echar a perder la carne) line and the local-Peruvian-plus-gringo-accent detail are her on-record account. Whether the attackers literally said this or her recollection under sedation/trauma reconstructed it cannot be independently confirmed. The event happened; the precise dialogue is single-witness testimony.

claims.text: The Ikitu tribal elders recognize the Pelacaras as a modern-tech version of the Pishtaco — a 500-year-old legend dating to the 1500s Spanish conquest, when conquistadors hunted Andean people for their body fat (which Europeans valued for treating wounds, greasing machines, and lubricating weapons), making the harvesting of fat a cultural desecration in Andean cosmology.

evidence.quote:
s, it
was both terrifying and familiar. They
knew this story. It had been passed down
through the tribe for 500 years.
The technology was new. The method of
attack wasn't. The Ikatu tribe elders
knew exactly what they were facing
because their ancestors had faced it
before. They had a name for it, Pishaco.
In the 1500s, when Spanish concistadors
tore through Peru with steel and
gunpowder, they were searching for gold.
Any locals that got in their way were
killed. But it got worse. The Spanish
were taking something from the bodies of
the dead. Something the Andian people
considered very valuable. Human fat. In
Europe, fat was used to treat wounds,
grease the gears of machines, maintain
weapons. But they believed that human
fat was even more potent. But for the
Andian people, this was a desecration.
In their culture, fat was life. To take
a person's fat was to steal their
essence. The pishtaco was born from this
horror. A pale-kinned man who hunted at
night, carried a knife, and peeled the
skin off his victims. Now, this isn't
just a ghost story. In 2009, Provian
police arrested a gang carrying plastic
soda bottles filled with liqufied human
fat. They were selling it on the
European black

claims.rationale: The Pishtaco / Nakaq / Kharisiri Andean folkloric figure is real and well-documented in anthropological literature (Mary Weismantel's 'Cholas and Pishtacos' is the standard ethnography). Origin in colonial-era Spanish conquest as a memory of European exploitation is the consensus reading. Human fat had documented medical and industrial uses in 16th-17th century Europe (e.g. Adipocera, Axungia hominis in apothecary records). The cultural significance of fat in Andean cosmology is also well-attested. Solid historical grounding.

claims.text: The Pishtaco legend isn't pure folklore — in 2009 Peruvian police arrested a real gang carrying soda bottles of liquefied human fat that they were selling on the European black market for use in high-end cosmetics at $36,000 a gallon.

evidence.quote:
market. Human fat was
supposedly used in high-end cosmetics.
The price, $36,000
a gallon. The ancient legend had found a
modern market. Evidence of the pistachos
kept appearing. A few days later, in the
village of Okabomba, farmers found all
their sheep dead. The injuries were
c

claims.rationale: The 2009 Peruvian National Police announcement of the 'Pishtacos gang' arrest in Huanuco province (alleging the killing of dozens for body fat) was a real news event widely reported. However, much of the case fell apart on subsequent investigation — Peruvian Interior Minister Octavio Salazar later admitted some of the dramatic claims (fat being sold to European cosmetics firms, $15,000-60,000/gallon prices) were not supported by evidence. The arrests happened; the cosmetics-supply-chain narrative was likely sensationalized.

claims.text: The official Peruvian government explanation — that the attackers were illegal gold miners using commercial jetpacks to scare villagers off their land — does not survive scrutiny: commercial jetpacks are loud and last minutes (not hours of silent hovering), the attacks deliberately attracted global attention (the opposite of what an illegal mining operation wants), and the nearby city of Iñapari with no gold also had hovering humanoid sightings.

evidence.quote:
igate. It didn't take them long to
solve the crime. They knew exactly what
this was. Miners wearing jetpacks.
The police investigation was brief. The
conclusion was simple. The attackers
were illegal gold miners using jetpacks
to scare the villagers off their land.
The international press picked up the
story. It was convenient explanation
that dismissed the entire phenomenon and
the explanation stuck. But the story,
the jetpack minor story had holes, big
ones.

moved with superhuman agility, leaping
over houses, and all in complete
silence. You couldn't do this in the
middle of the densest jungle in the
world.
The villagers had set traps around the
perimeter. Shotguns rigged trip wires.
Throughout July, they heard the traps
firing at night. Every morning, they'd
check for bodies. All they found were
mysterious oval footprints twice the
size of human feet and no blood.
And the motive doesn't make sense.
Cartels and illegal miners want to stay
under the radar. These attacks were
terrifying. They brought global
attention. The last thing a covert
mining operation wants is a visit from
the press or the police.
The jetpack story collapses completely
when you look at the city of NA nearby.
35,000 people and no gold. And yet, a
humanoid figure was caught on video
climbing a rooftop in the dead of night.
Why would a minor be doing that?
If these attacks weren't done by minors
or the cartels, then we'r

claims.rationale: The official jetpack-miners explanation is genuinely fragile on technical grounds: JetPack Aviation's commercial JB-series rigs have ~10-minute endurance and ~95dB sound output; the silent-multi-hour-hovering claim is incompatible with current commercial gear. The 'no gold near Iquitos / nearby cities also affected' geographical critique is also accurate. The host's reasoning here matches the actual epistemic gap in the official story.

claims.text: Roughly 12,000 women — mostly girls and teenagers — vanish in Peru every year and fewer than half are ever found, providing a real-world organ-trafficking and sex-trafficking context that, combined with the multi-billion-dollar black market for human organs (a single body worth millions), gives the face-peeler attacks a plausible economic motive.

evidence.quote:
e looking at
something far darker. Because every year
nearly 12,000 women vanish in Peru. Most
are girls, teenagers. Fewer than half
are ever found. Some are trafficked,
others completely disappear, but
disappear to where. Some investigators
believe these women are being used as
livestock.
The black market for human organs is a
multi-billion dollar industry. A single
body can be worth millions. The face
peeler motive suddenly had a terrifying
realworld context.
Organ harvesting explains the motive.
Remote villages provides the
opportunity. But what about the means?
This theory doesn't explain the
technology.

claims.rationale: Peru's Defensoría del Pueblo and Ministry of Women report 5,000-15,000 women/girls reported missing annually depending on year; the 'most never found' framing is consistent with the Defensoría's audits of the Alerta Amber system. The global black-market organ trade is real (WHO estimates ~10,000 illegal kidney transplants/year alone); body-as-millions valuation is in the right order of magnitude for full organ retrieval. The host's underlying epidemiological numbers are roughly accurate; the link to specifically the Pelacaras attacks is speculative.

claims.text: While the 2023 attacks were happening, the US military was running a massive joint-services exercise called Resolute Sentinel 2023 (including 87 Marines and even Space Force personnel) out of Iquitos naval base — just up-river from Tia's village; Marines were in the region until July 9, the Pelacaras attacks began July 11, suggesting the attackers may have been operators field-testing black-budget tech like Talos exoskeletons, ducted-fan/EM lift platforms, night vision and directed-energy weapons.

evidence.quote:
Gangs and cartels have a lot
of money, but not for technology like
this. If we're assuming these attacks
are being done by men and not aliens,
who would have such advanced tech? The
CIA and military might. So, was the US
military operating deep in the jungles
of Peru at the exact time of these
attacks?
They were.

organized, armed, American.

stories don't sound like aliens. They

definitely work on human skin. And then

Two voices, one local, the other a
gring

claims.rationale: Resolute Sentinel 2023 was a real US Southern Command joint exercise in Peru with multinational and multi-service participation including elements from the Marine Corps and Space Force; documented in DoD press releases. The temporal proximity (Marine departure ~July 9, Pelacaras attacks reported from July 11) is real and noteworthy. However: TALOS/Iron Man suit was officially shelved in 2019 without delivering a working operational model; silent EM-propulsion lift platforms remain at the laboratory stage. The link from real military exercise to attackers literally testing covert tech is speculative; the timeline coincidence is solid.

claims.text: American explorer Tim Alberino and former Marine Doug Thornton mounted a privately funded October 2023 expedition to San Antonio de Pintuyacu, providing supplies, training the villagers in patrol tactics and weapon safety, and concluding after dozens of witness interviews that the villagers were honest about what they saw — but were facing not aliens but a powerful international sex-trafficking and organ-harvesting organization using possibly reverse-engineered alien technology.

evidence.quote:
e of San
Antonio Depenty were on their own. Then
in October 2023, help arrived. American
explorer and investigator Tim Alberino
and former Marine Doug Thornton. They
launched a privately funded expedition
to the Amazon. Their mission to provide
aid and find the truth. They brought
supplies, food, and medicine. They
brought high-powered flashlights,
radios, night vision goggles with

Everybody was on edge. Men carried

they didn't have the experience or

He taught the men basic patrol tactics.
How to move in a column, how to handle

dozens of witnesses, Alberino was
convinced the villagers were telling the
truth about what they saw. But he didn't
believe they were seeing aliens. He
believed they

claims.rationale: Tim Alberino did mount the publicized October 2023 expedition with Doug Thornton; both have publicly documented their work in San Antonio de Pintuyacu and on Alberino's own platform. The aid delivery, weapon training, and witness interviews are documented in their own video releases and X posts. The conclusion that the witnesses were sincere (vs. fabricating) is Alberino's stated assessment. The 'reverse-engineered alien tech / international trafficking org' is Alberino's interpretation, not established fact.

claims.text: Faking supernatural attacks for counterinsurgency purposes is not far-fetched: the CIA's psychological warfare unit literally faked Aswang vampire attacks (kidnapping Hukbalahap fighters, puncturing their necks, and hanging corpses upside down to drain blood) in 1950s Philippines, and ran Operation Wandering Soul broadcasting ghostly voices into the Vietnam jungle.

evidence.quote:
but pretending to be face peelers sounds
like a stretch. It isn't.
The CIA once faked vampire attacks as
part of a counterinsurgency strategy. In
the 1950s in the Philippines, a
communist group called the Hooks tried
to overthrow the government. The CIA
brought in a psychological warfare unit
to try to scare the hucks away. There
was a local legend of a monster called
the Aswang. The Aswang had a sharp
tongue that it would use to drain the
blood of its victims. So, the CIA
started hunting the hooks. They
kidnapped them, punctured their necks,
and hung the corpses upside down to
drain their blood. The plan worked
better than they expected.
The same scops unit was deployed in
Vietnam a few years later for Operation
Wandering Soul. They broadcast ghostly
voices and screams into the jungle at
night. This was so effective that the US
was instructed not to play the sounds
around their local allies. They were
terrified.
[Music]
But w

claims.rationale: Edward Lansdale, in his 1972 memoir 'In the Midst of Wars', explicitly describes the Hukbalahap counterinsurgency aswang/asuang ruse in 1950s Philippines (puncturing necks of captured Huks and leaving the bodies to be found). This is one of the most-cited concrete examples of US PSYWAR mythologizing. Operation Wandering Soul / 'Ghost Tape Number 10' in Vietnam is also documented (US Army 6th PSYOP Battalion, 1969-71). Both anecdotes are historically solid.

claims.text: AJ rejects both the alien-attackers explanation and the official jetpack-miners cover story: whatever the attackers actually were (CIA operatives, soldiers, traffickers, miners), they were definitely organized and technologically superior to the villagers, the official story is a lie, and these face-peeler attacks are an industry being run by evil men on defenseless remote populations across South America and beyond.

evidence.quote:
rs later for Operation
Wandering Soul. They broadcast ghostly
voices and screams into the jungle at
night. This was so effective that the US
was instructed not to play the sounds
around their local allies. They were
terrified.
[Music]
But why would the CIA go through all
this trouble and take on so much risk
for money? They have access to all the
money they need.
And what about Tim Alberino's theory
about a shadowy organization harvesting
organs using alien technology? That's
probably the best theory we have, but
there's no proof to support it. But Tim
went to the jungle. He spoke to the
witnesses. He did prove their fear is
real. The official story is a lie. We
know that. Whether the attackers were
minors, soldiers, traffickers, or
something else, they were organized and
technologically superior.
I don't think this is aliens. I'm pretty
sure this is being done by men, evil
men. And if this is happening in Peru,
it's happening to defenseless people in
remote villages all over the continent,
maybe even beyond.
Advanced technology in the hands of
those who profit from suffering. We've
seen that all throughout history. The
face peelers aren't a legend anymore.
They're an industry and business is
booming.
[Music]
Thank you so much for hanging out with
me

claims.rationale: The verdict combines a defensible negative claim (the official story has technical holes) with a more speculative positive claim (organized international human-exploitation industry). The negative claim — official story doesn't add up — is supported by genuine technical inconsistencies. The positive claim — coordinated global industry — is consistent with the broader evidence on organ trafficking and remote-village exploitation but specific link to the Pelacaras attacks remains unproved. directTruth moderate to reflect the partial defensibility.

dependencies.rationale: Builds on the demolition of the official jetpack-miners explanation.
Connects to the trafficking-and-organ-harvesting motive analysis.
